metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Composition and Production 11 focuses on the craft of musical composition and audio production. Students develop skills in melody and harmonic writing, arranging for various ensembles, and using digital audio workstations (DAWs) to compose, produce, and mix original music. The course bridges traditional music theory and contemporary music technology — preparing students for post-secondary music production, composition, and music technology programs. First Peoples musical traditions and contemporary Indigenous artists are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A senior music composition and technology course developing skills in melody writing, harmonic composition, music arranging, and digital audio production using DAWs — bridging traditional music theory with contemporary music creation.

big_ideas.statement: Composition and production are forms of artistic inquiry and personal expression.
big_ideas.elaboration: Writing and producing music is a form of thinking — exploring ideas through sound, making choices about what to include and exclude, and shaping sonic experiences for listeners.

big_ideas.statement: Musical structure and technology are tools for artistic communication.
big_ideas.elaboration: Harmony, melody, rhythm, texture, and form are not constraints on creativity — they are the materials composers use to build musical meaning. Technology expands these possibilities.

big_ideas.statement: Music reflects cultural values and creates community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every musical style and production tradition carries cultural meaning. Understanding this context is essential to creating music that communicates authentically and respectfully.

competencies.raw: Develop composition and production skills using notation and digital tools; create original musical works in diverse styles; experiment with musical forms, textures, and sounds.
i_can_statements: I can compose original melodies, harmonizations, and short works using notation software or DAW.
I can create music in multiple styles — cinematic, electronic, pop, jazz, or contemporary classical.
I can use a DAW to record, layer, mix, and produce original music.
I can experiment with form, texture, and timbre to shape musical meaning.

competencies.raw: Analyze compositions using music theory vocabulary; apply feedback to refine compositional work; examine how composition choices create specific musical effects.
i_can_statements: I can analyze musical compositions using music theory: form, harmony, melody, texture, and rhythm.
I can apply feedback from peers and instructors to refine and strengthen my compositions.
I can examine how compositional choices — instrumentation, dynamics, texture — create specific emotional and aesthetic effects.

competencies.raw: Share original compositions and productions; document creative process; present compositional decisions to peers and audiences.
i_can_statements: I can share original compositions and productions for peer review and public presentation.
I can document my compositional process through project journals, revision drafts, and audio logs.
I can present and explain my compositional decisions to peers and audiences.

competencies.raw: Respect copyright and cultural protocols in composition; engage with First Peoples compositional and musical traditions; connect music creation to community and cultural purposes.
i_can_statements: I can respect copyright, sampling ethics, and cultural protocols in all compositional work.
I can engage respectfully with First Peoples compositional and musical traditions.
I can create music for community purposes — film scores, events, social commentary — connecting composition to real-world needs.

areas.name: Music Theory for Composition
areas.raw: Harmonic and melodic writing; music theory applied to original composition.
topics: Melody writing: contour, rhythm, phrase structure, and motivic development
Diatonic harmony: triads, seventh chords, and functional progression
Chord substitution and borrowed chords: adding colour to harmony
Voice leading principles: part writing for 2-4 voices
Introduction to modal and non-functional harmony
Rhythmic complexity: syncopation, polyrhythm, and metric modulation

areas.name: Arranging and Orchestration
areas.raw: Arranging for small and large ensembles; orchestration principles.
topics: Instrument ranges and transpositions
Arranging for rhythm section: piano, bass, guitar, and drums
Arranging for small combo: jazz, pop, and rock arranging conventions
String and wind writing fundamentals
Choral arranging: voicing, doubling, and textural variation
Score preparation and notation conventions

areas.name: Digital Audio Production
areas.raw: DAW production: recording, editing, mixing, and mastering.
topics: DAW fundamentals: session setup, MIDI and audio tracks, routing
MIDI composition: programming instruments, quantization, and humanization
Audio recording: microphone technique, gain staging, and signal flow
Editing: arranging clips, time-stretching, and pitch correction
Mixing: EQ, compression, reverb, delay, and basic signal processing
Mastering basics: loudness, limiting, and export formats

areas.name: Musical Styles and Creative Projects
areas.raw: Composing in diverse styles; First Peoples music and creative projects.
topics: Film and game scoring: composing to picture, sync points, and mood matching
Electronic and ambient music: sound design and texture-based composition
First Peoples musical traditions and contemporary Indigenous composers
Song production: producing a complete original track in a chosen genre
Portfolio project: completing and presenting a substantial composition or EP
